title: Wii U
content:
Iwata Asks HomeWii U
PlatinumGames
1. Culture Shock2. Into the Trash3. The Dream of Being a Game Creator4. Valuing the Players5. High Quality6. Made in Japan
Culture ShockMinamiThank you for coming.IwataNot at all. It's such a nice office.MinamiThank you. (laughs)IwataToday, I'm here to conduct a new challenge. Most sessions of "Iwata Asks" so far have dealt with a specific software or hardware, but since we have become closely acquainted withPlatinumGames.1, I thought it would be good if I could talk about PlatinumGames as a development group, rather than about a specific product, and I asked for your time. I'm not sure how this will turn out, but I'm looking forward to it. Thank you.
1. PlatinumGames Inc.: A video game developer founded in 2006. The company is currently developing The Wonderful 101 and Bayonetta 2 games for the Wii U console. Head office: Osaka.
Minami and InabaThank you!IwataYou're both prominent figures in the gaming world so I'm sure many of the readers would know who you are, but would you please introduce yourselves?MinamiI'm Minami, president and CEO of PlatinumGames. I've been in the industry for about 25 years. First, I was involved in development at a publisher company for a long time, but about seven years ago, for various reasons, I established PlatinumGames.IwataWhat were those reasons?MinamiThe biggest reason for founding the company was that I wanted to create a group that specializes in making something. I want to achieve as an independent developer what can't be done at a large organization such as a publisher. In order to make this distinctive group of creators into a world-class team, I've stepped back from the development work in order to focus on representing the company.IwataThank you. Inaba-san?InabaI'm Inaba from PlatinumGames. Like Minami-san, I came to this industry from a publisher, and about 20 years have passed. I was originally a programmer, but since becoming a producer, it's been about ten years. I've mostly produced original titles.IwataIt isn't often that one gets to always take on original titles the way you do.InabaI think that's because it's my personality to always seek new stimulation. But in seeking stimulation, I'm willful and make all kinds of stuff, so when it comes to figuring out how to turn what I make into products, I suspect Minami-san has quite a hard time behind the scenes! (laughs)MinamiNo, that's not true. (laughs)IwataI suppose that's because you share an understanding of each of your roles and sort of role-play.2
2. Role-playing: Performing a set role.
InabaYes. And as a producer, directors make lots of demands of you, so you have to release that stress somewhere. Minami-san the CEO just happens to be above me, so I do that with him. I don't know where he goes to work out stress, though! (laughs)Minami(laughs)IwataOkay, first I'd like to ask about how you first encountered video games. About the time we started making video games, the industry wasn't as large as it is now, so I imagine that, including that encounter, there were a lot of interesting twists and turns. Minami-san, where did you first encounter video games?MinamiI'll turn 50 this year, so my memories of my youth are of arcade games. At the time, Space Invaders3 and Donkey Kong4 were popular, so I was playing games like that at the arcades. But I was just a player then and didn't link it to an occupation at all.
3. Space Invaders: A shooting game that appeared in arcades in 1978.
4. Donkey Kong: An arcade game that appeared in 1981. The Famicom (NES in Japan) version was released simultaneously with that system in July 1983.
IwataIn other words, it didn't occur to you that video game companies might be a place to work.MinamiRight. At the time, there was nothing like video game technical schools, so the path to working in video games wasn't as clearly laid out as it is today.IwataEven to people who fervently frequented video arcades, it was a mystery who was making the games they played and how they were making them.MinamiYes. So I never imagined making video games as a job. Then, after graduating from high school, I started attending design school. One time, my peers and I had to work on a group project. They gathered at my house, and one of my friends brought over an NES so we could play with it during breaks.IwataOh, that's not a good sign! (laughs)MinamiIt wasn't! (laughs) That's when I first encountered the Super Mario Bros. game. Instead of just using it for quick breaks, we got absorbed in it!IwataAnd thanks to that, you didn't make any progress on your project, right? (laughs)MinamiBut we made progress on Super Mario! (laughs) That's how I encountered home video games, but even at the time, I had no thoughts of getting a job in the game industry.IwataHow did you come to work at a video game company then?MinamiI was studying graphic design at design school and dabbling in things like early computer graphics, and I was also incredibly interested in computers. So when I looked for a job, my criteria were, above all, a company where I could use a computer, and a company with the best conditions as possible. I found three in the Kansai area and they were all video game companies.IwataI see.MinamiThat's how I came to work at Capcom. The thing that surprised me most when I entered the company was how they were creating pictures with pixels. When I started, I was like, "Huh?! What's that?!"IwataThere are similarities between pixel art and drawing with traditional art materials, but the work is largely different.MinamiCompletely different. That was the biggest culture shock.IwataWhat work were you first involved with?MinamiWhen I joined the company, Capcom was mainly an arcade game company. I was in their department that worked on consumer games, so the first job I did was to take an arcade title andâ¦IwataPort it.MinamiYes. For the first year, I studied the consumer titles that other companies were making, and as a designer, I created pixel art. But at some point I realized I didn't have a talent for it.IwataIt's surprising to hear someone like you say you don't have talent. You mean you just weren't suited for pixelated art?MinamiRight. So I consulted my boss, and he said, "Well then, how about planning?"IwataAnd that was a turning point in your life.MinamiYes. I guess that was a little more suitable, and it led to me being a project planner, director, and then producer.
Iwata Asks HomeNext
interviewWii U: PlatinumGames00
